title: Thermodynamics of classifiers
abstract: The Landauer principle bridges the energetic cost and information processing, showing that irreversible computation inevitably demands energy dissipation. As energy demands from computation continue to rise, approximate computing has attracted considerable attention. Approximate computing is based on the idea that energy consumption can be reduced by sacrificing computational accuracy. This raises a fundamental question about the relationship between error and thermodynamic cost in information processing. In this study, we derive the error-cost trade-off in the binary classifier by considering classification based on Markov processes. We obtain the lower bounds on the Bayes error in terms of thermodynamic costs such as entropy production and dynamical activity. Our results show that when entropy production or dynamical activity vanishes, the Bayes error reaches $1/2$, equivalent to random guessing, while greater thermodynamic costs enable lower error. This establishes a fundamental trade-off between error and cost in information processing by thermodynamic systems. Because the Bayes error provides the lowest achievable error among all possible classifiers, the classification error cannot fall below the obtained bounds given the entropy production or dynamical activity. We also discuss the quantum generalization and show that the Bayes error of the quantum classifier is bounded from below by the variance of the Hamiltonian.
summary: This paper establishes fundamental lower bounds on the minimum error achievable in binary classification tasks based on thermodynamic quantities. It proves that reducing classification error requires increasing thermodynamic costs like entropy production. The results extend from classical Markov processes to quantum systems, relating error to Hamiltonian variance.
main_problem: Establishing a fundamental thermodynamic trade-off between classification error (Bayes error) and thermodynamic costs such as entropy production and dynamical activity.
main_result: The study derives lower bounds on the Bayes error in binary classification, showing that higher thermodynamic costs enable lower error, while vanishing costs lead to random guessing (error of 1/2).
method: The author uses stochastic thermodynamics, information theory (Bhattacharyya coefficient, Pinsker inequality), and the Cramér-Rao inequality to link precision to dissipation.
model_or_system: The models include continuous-time Markov processes on finite state spaces for classical classification and closed quantum systems evolving under class-dependent Hamiltonians for quantum classification.
key_observables: Bayes error (P_err), entropy production (Sigma), dynamical activity (A), and Hamiltonian variance.
important_parameters: The limits where entropy production or dynamical activity vanish, and the regime of uniform priors.
main_assumptions: Assumes local detailed balance for Markov processes, identical initial states for both classes, and uncoupled dynamics for the two-process construction.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides schematic illustrations of four classification settings: classical machine learning, Markov process state-based, Markov process trajectory-based, and quantum state discrimination.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of error-cost trade-offs, develops classical bounds using Markov processes and entropy/activity, extends these to trajectory-based classification, provides a quantum generalization via the Helstrom bound, and concludes with mathematical appendices for detailed derivations.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental link between information processing accuracy and physical dissipation, which is highly relevant for understanding the thermodynamic limits of open quantum systems and the precision of quantum state discrimination.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of binary classification. (a) Classical setting: the classifier predicts the label ˆY (x) ∈ {0, 1} from an input sample x drawn from overlapping class- conditional distributions corresponding to Y = 0 and Y = 1. (b) Markov process state setting. The classifier predicts the label from the final state of the Markov process. (c) Markov process trajectory setting. The classifier predicts the label from a trajectory drawn from the Markov process. (d) Quan- tum setting: the classifier performs a measurement Π0, Π1 on an input quantum state |ψ0⟩or |ψ1⟩and outputs the predicted label ˆY ∈{0, 1}.
